summary:
The presence of intra-epithelial blisters indicates a specific type of autoimmune disease affecting the epidermis and mucous membranes where antibodies target cell-adhesion molecules leading to separation within layers of the epithelium.
Intradermal blisters would be more typical for conditions such as toxic shock syndrome or drug reactions rather than being associated with an immune-mediated process targeting intercellular adhesions specifically at the level of the epidermis/mucosa interface. Agent2 reasoned that based on this understanding, options not related directly to these mechanisms could likely be ruled out when identifying causes linked explicitly to intra-epithelial structures forming due to immunological processes disrupting normal cellular cohesion. To summarize:

The finding of intra-epithelial bullae suggests involvement in autoimmune blistering disorders characterized by antibody-induced disruption of desmosomal connections among keratinocytes; other types of blisters typically occur deeper dermally without involving uppermost intact stratified squamous epithelia interfaces normally targeted here clinically.
